<!-- Auto-generated by: ./scripts/refresh-transcript.sh
     Source: ./scripts/demo.sh --no-pause
     Do not edit by hand — re-run this script to refresh.
     Machine-specific paths normalized to portable placeholders. -->

codex-mcp-doctor — guided demo
python: Python 3.14.6
repo:   <repo-root>

================================================================
  SCENE 1 — The Silent Failure
================================================================
MCP servers fail silently. config.toml looks fine, Codex starts, but zero tools appear. No error, no log.

# Example: broken stdio MCP server config
# This server's command path doesn't exist — doctor will catch it at L2 (config validation)
[mcp_servers.broken-path]
command = "/usr/local/bin/nonexistent-mcp-server"
args = ["--port", "8080"]

[mcp_servers.broken-env]
command = "python3"
args = ["-m", "my_missing_mcp_server"]

[mcp_servers.broken-env.env]
DATABASE_URL = "postgresql://localhost:9999/nonexistent"

================================================================
  SCENE 2 — The Doctor Diagnoses
================================================================
One command, zero dependencies, root-causes both broken servers in under a second.

$ python3 scripts/doctor.py --config examples/broken-stdio/config.toml
================================================================
  MCP DOCTOR - Diagnostic Report
================================================================
  Config: examples/broken-stdio/config.toml

  Servers: 2 total  ✅ 0 healthy  ❌ 2 errors  🔴 avg score 0.0

  ❌ broken-path  🔴 0.0
     transport: stdio  |  0 tools
     ❌ [command_not_found] Command path does not exist: /usr/local/bin/nonexistent-mcp-server
        → fix: Verify the path or reinstall the MCP server. Check if the binary moved.
     ℹ️  [probe_skipped] Skipping connectivity probe due to config errors above.

  ❌ broken-env  🔴 0.0
     transport: stdio  |  0 tools (33ms)
     ❌ [process_crashed] Server process exited with code 1.
        → fix: A Python dependency is missing. Reinstall the MCP server in its virtualenv.
        stderr: python3: No module named my_missing_mcp_server

================================================================
  RESULT: 2 error(s), 0 warning(s) - issues found.
  Fix the errors above, then re-run: python3 scripts/doctor.py
================================================================

================================================================
  SCENE 3 — Security: Supply Chain + Secrets
================================================================
Silently hostile servers: an unpinned npx package can pull a compromised update; a plaintext API key in http_headers leaks if config.toml is committed.

$ python3 scripts/doctor.py --config examples/security-issues/config.toml --check secrets --skip-probe
================================================================
  MCP DOCTOR - Diagnostic Report
================================================================
  Config: examples/security-issues/config.toml

  Servers: 2 total  ✅ 0 healthy  ⚠️  2 warnings  🟢 avg score 90.0

  ⚠️  unpinned-npx  🟢 90.0
     transport: stdio  |  0 tools
     ⚠️  [unpinned_package] Server 'unpinned-npx' uses 'some-mcp-server' without a version pin.
        → fix: Pin to a concrete version, e.g. some-mcp-server@1.2.3, so a republished package can't silently change behavior.

  ⚠️  plaintext-secret  🟢 90.0
     transport: http  |  0 tools
     ⚠️  [plaintext_secret_header] Server 'plaintext-secret' has a hardcoded secret in http_headers['Authorization'].
        → fix: Prefer bearer_token_env_var (Codex resolves $VAR at launch) over a literal token in http_headers; if you must inline, ensure config.toml is gitignored.

================================================================
  RESULT: 2 warning(s) - servers running but check the warnings.
================================================================

================================================================
  SCENE 3b — W022 Cyrillic Homoglyph Attack
================================================================
A tool whose name LOOKS like 'filesystem_read' — but the 'e' is Cyrillic U+0435. Passes any human code review. First MCP scanner to catch this.

$ python3 scripts/doctor.py --config examples/homoglyph-attack/config.toml
================================================================
  MCP DOCTOR - Diagnostic Report
================================================================
  Config: examples/homoglyph-attack/config.toml

  Servers: 1 total  ✅ 0 healthy  ⚠️  1 warnings  🟡 avg score 50.0

  ⚠️  poisoned-fs  🟡 50.0
     transport: stdio  |  1 tools (24ms)
     server: poisoned-fs v0.1.0
     protocol: 2024-11-05 [no capabilities]
     tools: filеsystem_read
     security: 🔴 1 high
       🔴 [W022] Tool 'filеsystem_read' contains mixed-script word 'filеsystem_read' with Cyrillic lookalikes (U+0435). Normalizes to 'filesystem_read'.
          evidence: filеsystem_read
          → fix: Replace Cyrillic lookalike characters with their ASCII equivalents in tool names and descriptions.

================================================================
  RESULT: 1 warning(s) - servers running but check the warnings.
================================================================

================================================================
  SCENE 4 — Rug-Pull Detection (E003)
================================================================
The most dangerous attack: a tool description that changes silently after you trusted it. First CLI implementation of tool-description pinning. (Web-only at Invariant Labs' MCP-Scan.)


Step 1 — pin the trusted baseline (one tool, sha256 of its description):
$ python3 scripts/doctor.py --config examples/homoglyph-attack/config.toml --save-baseline --baseline-path /tmp/baseline.json --quiet
Baseline saved: /tmp/baseline.json (1 tools)

Baseline contents:
{
  "poisoned-fs": {
    "fil\u0435system_read": "e5d6013ea6f5acb3ef658e031c2ac348b7b1424eca606e13dde4ee529100c37c"
  }
}
Step 2 — simulate the attack. The server silently changes its tool's description.
We corrupt the stored hash to represent a tampered description, then re-check:
  → baseline hash corrupted (simulating server-side description mutation)

Step 3 — re-check against the baseline. The doctor must flag E003 rug-pull:
$ python3 scripts/doctor.py --config examples/homoglyph-attack/config.toml --check-baseline --baseline-path /tmp/baseline.json
================================================================
  MCP DOCTOR - Diagnostic Report
================================================================
  Config: examples/homoglyph-attack/config.toml

  Servers: 1 total  ✅ 0 healthy  ⚠️  1 warnings  🟡 avg score 50.0

  ⚠️  poisoned-fs  🟡 50.0
     transport: stdio  |  1 tools (34ms)
     server: poisoned-fs v0.1.0
     protocol: 2024-11-05 [no capabilities]
     tools: filеsystem_read
     security: 🔴 2 high
       🔴 [W022] Tool 'filеsystem_read' contains mixed-script word 'filеsystem_read' with Cyrillic lookalikes (U+0435). Normalizes to 'filesystem_read'.
          evidence: filеsystem_read
          → fix: Replace Cyrillic lookalike characters with their ASCII equivalents in tool names and descriptions.
       🔴 [E003] Tool 'poisoned-fs:filеsystem_read' description changed since the last baseline - possible rug-pull. Re-run with --save-baseline only after verifying the new description is safe.
          evidence: description hash mismatch
          → fix: Verify the tool description change is intentional. Re-run --save-baseline after confirming safety.

================================================================
  RESULT: 1 warning(s) - servers running but check the warnings.
================================================================

================================================================
  SCENE 5 — Auto-Triggering Hook
================================================================
Ships with a Codex SessionStart hook (hooks/hooks.json). If your MCP setup is healthy, completely silent. If something broke, you see it before your first prompt — not after 20 minutes of debugging.


hooks/hooks.json:
{
  "hooks": {
    "SessionStart": [
      {
        "hooks": [
          {
            "type": "command",
            "command": "sh \"${PLUGIN_ROOT}/hooks/hook.sh\" 2>/dev/null || true",
            "timeout": 10
          }
        ]
      }
    ]
  }
}

Try it locally:
  cp hooks/hooks.json ~/.codex/hooks.json
  # then start a new Codex session

================================================================
  END OF DEMO
  266 tests · 0 dependencies · pure Python 3.11+ stdlib
  https://github.com/luogangan7-lgtm/codex-mcp-doctor
================================================================
